text: Iliotibial band syndrome (ITBS) is the most common injury of the lateral side of the knee in runners, with an incidence estimated between 5% and 14%.
Hip, knee, and ankle/foot kinetics and kinematics are considerably different in runners with ITBS compared to those without, suggesting biomechanical factors play a key role.
Evidence is limited and conflicting on whether hip abductor weakness has a major role in the development of ITBS in runners.
Hip/knee coordination and running style appear to be key factors in the treatment of ITBS in runners.
Runners with ITBS may benefit from mobilization, hip strengthening exercises, and advice about running shoes and running surface.
Clinical tests commonly used to diagnose and monitor ITBS have not been validated for runners with this condition.
